coredata.dc:description: Hydrogels have a significant impact on the fields of biological study and medical diagnosis. They are becoming more useful in bioanalytical and biosensing applications. The intriguing new nanomaterials quantum dots-hydrogel composites have gained a lot of interest because of their unmatched biocompatibility and tolerable biodegradability, which opens up a wide range of possible applications. Focusing on synthesis techniques, this review describes current developments in quantum dots-hydrogel composites, such as hydrogel gelation in quantum dots (QDs) solution, inserting prepared QDs into hydrogels after gelation, generating QDs in situ inside the preformed gel, and cross-linking through QDs. Biomedical applications such as bioimaging and biosensing are specifically examined, and then the inherent problems of design optimisation, biocompatibility, and bimodal applications, as well as the potential of future development, are discussed.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent quantum dots-based hydrogels: Synthesis, fabrication and multimodal biosensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Bar-Ilan Institute for Nanotechnology and Advanced Materials (BINA), Bar-Ilan University, Ramat-Gan 5290002, Israel

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Bar-Ilan Institute for Nanotechnology and Advanced Materials (BINA)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Bar-Ilan Institute for Nanotechnology and Advanced Materials (BINA), Bar-Ilan University, Ramat-Gan 5290002, Israel

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrogels have a significant impact on the fields of biological study and medical diagnosis. They are becoming more useful in bioanalytical and biosensing applications. The intriguing new nanomaterials quantum dots-hydrogel composites have gained a lot of interest because of their unmatched biocompatibility and tolerable biodegradability, which opens up a wide range of possible applications. Focusing on synthesis techniques, this review describes current developments in quantum dots-hydrogel composites, such as hydrogel gelation in quantum dots (QDs) solution, inserting prepared QDs into hydrogels after gelation, generating QDs in situ inside the preformed gel, and cross-linking through QDs. Biomedical applications such as bioimaging and biosensing are specifically examined, and then the inherent problems of design optimisation, biocompatibility, and bimodal applications, as well as the potential of future development, are discussed.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The cross-linking of hydrophilic polymers results in the formation of three-dimensional networks known as hydrogels [1]. The densely cross-linked polymer chains in the structure of the hydrogel generated unique cells that resembled extracellular matrix. This led to remarkable performances of hydrogels, such as a high capacity for water retention and good tunability of mechanical, chemical, and biological characteristics [2–4]. Hydrogels have become attractive materials in the field of biomedicine due to the remarkable characteristics that they possess [5]. These characteristics include the ability to modify their physical, chemical, and biological properties; high biocompatibility; versatility in fabrication; and similarity to native extracellular matrix (ECM) [6,7]. There has been significant development in the synthesis and manufacturing of hydrogels for use in a variety of applications, including regenerative medicine, drug and gene delivery, stem cell and cancer research, and cell therapy [8–10]. These hydrogels can be created from either natural or synthetic sources. Hydrogels made from naturally occurring substances like collagen [11], chitosan [12], hyaluronic acid (HA) [13–15], alginate [16,17], gelatine [18], elastin [19], chondroitin sulphate [20], and heparin [21] are attractive for biological applications because of their cell signalling and cell-interactive characteristics, as well as their biodegradability [22]. Its possible drawbacks include immunogenicity, poor mechanical characteristics, and uncontrollable disintegration and structure [23]. Hydrogels may be tailored to suit functional needs via the use of a variety of cross-linking techniques [24]. Hence, hydrogels may also be sorted according to the type of cross-linking used to fabricate them [25]. Non-covalent bond interactions (such as ionic/electrostatic interactions, hydrophobic associations, hydrogen bonds, metal coordination, crystallisation, - stacking, etc.) between polymer chains or entanglements within the polymer network are typically responsible for the formation of physically cross-linked hydrogels [26–29].

Besides hydrogel based sensing, optical sensros are quite common also. Optical multimodal biosensing is a new discipline that combines different optical modalities to improve biosensing sensitivity, specificity, and information richness [30]. Fluorescence, SPR, Raman, absorbence, OCT, and SERS have been investigated for optical multimodal biosensing. Biomolecular interactions are studied using fluorescence biosensing. SPR provides label-free biomolecular interaction data by measuring refractive index near a metal film [31]. Raman spectroscopy uses vibrational fingerprints to identify biomolecules without labels. Light absorption determines analyte concentration in absorbence spectroscopy [32]. OCT delivers high-resolution cross-sectional pictures of biological tissues for imaging. SERS enhances Raman signals using nanostructured metal surfaces for ultrasensitive detection. These and other optical modalities offer potential biosensing capabilities and various uses in biological research, diagnostics, and environmental monitoring [33]. Multimode fibre (MMF)-hollow core fibre (HCF)-multimode fibre (MMF) optical fibre surface plasmon resonance (SPR) biosensor for in situ DNA hybridization investigation [34]. These sensors are sandwiched model where AuNPs are the main sensory probe operated at even 1 pM. Immobilising silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) functionalized with l-tyrosine on a polyethylene terephthalate (PET) substrate allowed for the development of a recyclable optical nanosensor for the quick measurement of Pb2+ ions [35]. Blood glucose level detector especially the sotbitol was also detected by optical detector based diagnostic system [36]. The sorbitol concentrations in both blood and urine were verified to be within the calibration range of the developed biosensor by the Mitsubayashi group. Optical detection by plasmic nanoparticles based systems were also reported where Mg2+ ions were determined in the dynamic range of 1–200 µm [37]. They developed l-tryptophan functionalized AgNPs which showed excellent selectivity towards Mg2+ ions even quite lower than human serum concentration. The bright future of luminescent materials is fluorescent hydrogels (FHs), which are hydrophilic polymer networks that are crosslinked in three dimensions [29]. Their luminescent properties may be adjusted [38]. Traditional fluorescent materials are often employed as dry solids or in solution, but these newer ones may also be utilised in both of these forms [39–41]. Water-swollen FHs exhibit desirable characteristics in both the solid and solution phases, indicating vast promise in a wide range of applications. Its luminescent sensors, displays, information encryption, etc. might all benefit from their crosslinked 3D hydrophilic networks, which could greatly enhance substance exchange with a surrounding aquatic environment to create a stunning fluorescence response [42]. Smart fluorescent hydrogels are a "rising star" in the field of optical materials. They have been used in both basic and applied research for fluorescent sensors, probes, imaging agents, photocatalysts, and light-emitting devices because of their unique ability to change colour in absorption (chromogenic) or emission (fluorochromic) in response to external stimuli like humidity, temperature, pH, light, ionic strength, and mechanical force. When materials are broken down into tiny particles, their physical properties can change in a number of important ways. Besides conventional nanomaterials [43–47], QDs, carbon nanotubes, aggregation-induced emission dyes, small molecule fluorophores, and semiconducting oligomer/polymer nanoparticles are just a few examples of the many optical nanomaterials with light absorption and conversion capabilities that have become hotspot topics in the fields of biological imaging, diagnosis, and sensing as chemistry and nanotechnology have advanced [46,48,49]. Numerous analytical studies over the past decade have focused on creating sensitive and widely applicable metallic ion sensors, such as those based on oligonucleotides [50], DNA [51], fluorophores [52] or chromophores [53], and nanoparticles [54–59]. Carbon nanotubes are ideal for gas, bio, and chemical sensors because to their high electrical conductivity, surface area, and mechanical robustness [60]. Anti-Stokes luminescence permits up conversion nanoparticles to convert low-energy excitation into higher-energy emission, providing sensitive biological and environmental monitoring. Alloyed nanoparticles like semiconductor QDs have variable optical characteristics, making them suitable for fluorescence-based sensors that detect analytes with high sensitivity and selectivity [61]. Surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) and localised surface plasmon resonance (LSPR)-based sensors may detect analytes with ultrasensitivity using metallic nanoparticles like gold and silver [62]. These nanoparticles can improve sensor sensitivity, selectivity, and signal amplification, advancing healthcare, environmental monitoring, and food safety. QDs with sizes less than 10 nm, a new type of luminous nanomaterials, have attracted a great deal of interest due to their exceptional and desirable features [63,64]. The advancement of their application in sensing and other disciplines is directly tied to the massive amount of scientific research and remarkable progress achieved on the synthesis, physical and optical characteristics of QDs [65–69]. Because of this, several studies in recent years have concentrated on finding ways to combine many luminogens into a single hydrogel matrix to create polymeric hydrogels that glow in a wide range of colours.

FHs can be fabricated with a simultaneous change in emission strength and colour as a result of the potential regulated overlap of the fluorescence spectra of several luminogens. Many QD uses, however, will not be fully realised until the QDs' surfaces are chemically engineered or they are embedded in a solid matrix [70]. It appears that including QDs into the 3D hydrogel network is a win-win situation. Hydrogels have the capacity to provide QDs both mechanical and chemical stability. Conversely, QDs introduced into hydrogels enhance the material's structure and performance. Fluorescent QDs tend to aggregate at high concentrations or in solid forms, which is seen as a limiting feature that has a major impact on their photoluminescence (PL) behaviour. The hydrogels' adapTable 3D porous structure and large surface area allow them to house a wide variety of micro/nano particles, which in turn eliminates QD clustering and significantly boosts the stability of the QDs' fluorescence. Through this Review, we want to stimulate fresh ideas, inspire new endeavours, and attract other scholars with similar interests to the developing field of FHs. It is important to emphasise that this Review is limited to multicolour fluorescent hydrogels made by physical or chemical crosslinking of hydrophilic synthetic or natural polymers and their multimodalities in sensitivities.

The field of fluorescent QDs have witnessed significant progress, leading to the emergence of novel biosensing platforms based on hydrogels. The present article provides an extensive investigation of the synthesis, fabrication, and multimodal biosensing functionalities of hydrogels based on fluorescent QDs [29,71,72]. The synthesis of hydrogels incorporated with QDs is a significant advancement in the field. Several techniques have been utilised to attain a uniform and regulated dispersion of QDs within the hydrogel matrix. Furthermore, there has been significant advancement in the fabrication methods of hydrogels based on QDs, with innovative techniques facilitating the development of hydrogel structures that are both highly stable and biocompatible. This review elucidates the current trend of multimodal biosensing, wherein QD-based hydrogels are integrated with other sensing modalities, including electrochemical, optical, or magnetic techniques, to augment the sensitivity, selectivity, and versatility of biosensing platforms. The amalgamation of QDs and hydrogels presents promising prospects for the development of sophisticated biosensors that exhibit enhanced efficacy and extensive versatility. The present review is a significant reference for researchers and scientists who aim to comprehend the recent advancements in the synthesis, fabrication, and multimodal biosensing capabilities of fluorescent QDs-based hydrogels. The ultimate goal of this research is to contribute to the progress of biosensing technologies in various fields, including healthcare, environmental monitoring, and food safety.

Since the use of QDs in biological diagnosis and imaging, optoelectronics, and sensors has become more widespread, researchers have become increasingly interested in developing methods to create QDs/hydrogel hybrid materials with customizable optical properties [73,74]. The synthesis of QDs is a process that involves the nucleation and growth of crystals, and the construction of hydrogel is a procedure that involves the physical or chemical cross-linking of polymeric chains in order to obtain a three-dimensional network structure [75]. Both of these processes are individual steps in the formation of two components. This molecule is responsible for an intriguing relationship between two processes that couldn't be more dissimilar to one another. In practise, however, researchers have run across a number of challenges, such as the poor compatibility of QDs with hydrogels and the degradation of the optical or electrical properties of QDs after they are linked to hydrogels. As a result, the development of dependable technologies for encapsulating QDs within hydrogel matrix is of utmost importance. In this article, we discuss the most recent synthetic approach of producing QDs/hydrogel hybrids in conjunction with the preparative order of the ingredients, which is a perspective that is substantially more applicable in nature. Fig. 1 provides a diagrammatic depiction of these three different approaches.

The formation of hydrogels in a QDs solution is the method that results in the most straightforward acquisition of QDs-hydrogel composites. Microfluidic channels and microparticles of poly(2-hydroxyethyl methacrylate) (PHEMA) hydrogel containing tyrosinase-QDs conjugates were combined to create a microdevice for the identification of phenol [76]. Dispersion photopolymerization was used to create PHEMA hydrogel microparticles (Fig. 2a ) carrying conjugates of enzyme (tyrosinase) and QD, which were then entrapped in a reaction chamber in a microfluidic channel including a microfilter. Fluorescence quenching effect generated by enzyme interaction between phenol and tyrosinase was employed to detect phenol.

Zhou et al. created a CdTe QD-embedded hydrogel that is both highly luminous and selectively fluoride-responsive [77]. Ion-exchange functionalized CdTe QDs showed a quick sol-gel transition and a selective reaction towards fluoride in aqueous solutions (Fig. 2b). The siloxane group exhibits a notable susceptibility to fluoride ion, leading to a rapid sol-gel transition upon its induction. Following the process of hydrogelation, it is possible to incorporate QDs within the resulting hydrogel matrix. For the purpose of detecting polyaromatic compounds, a method has been adopted to integrate GQDs into hydrogels via the process of in situ gelation [79]. Macroscopic fluorescent scaffolds made from GQD-based hydrogels that can be readily converted into aerogels have been depicted in Fig. 2c. The process for making GQDs was entirely environmentally friendly. The hydrogel is a cationic network made with N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA) as a cross-linker monomer and [2-(acryloyloxy)ethyl]trimethyl-ammonium chloride (AETA) as a radical initiator. For the GQDs, the positively charged nitrogen served as an anchor. Materials' fluorescence qualities were investigated. The GQDs' apparent quasi-independent emission (em) from the excitation wavelength (ex) is conserved at the macroscopic level.

To produce composites of QDs and hydrogel, an additional stage in the procedure entails the generation of QDs within the pre-existing gel. The proposed technique involves the initial loading of precursors into the hydrogel, rather than the primary embedding of the resulting QDs within the hydrogel matrix. Another method for producing CdS QDs by employing a hydrogel network as a template was reported elsewhere [80]. A typical procedure begins with the production of a crosslinked hydrophilic hydrogel through the use of 2-acrylamido-2-methyl-1-propansulonic acid (AMPS) monomers and the cross-linker N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide. This step is accomplished by the photo-polymerization approach. Q-dots generated on-site were extracted from hydrogel matrices by dissolving the p(AMPS)-CdS composite in distilled water. A technique using irradiation-induced reduction and polymerization-crosslinking was reported as a means of increasing the longevity and stability of QDs in the composite hydrogel [81]. Irradiation-induced polymerization-crosslinking was used to successfully manufacture CdS/P(AAc-co-AAm) composite hydrogel. The creation of CdS QDs and the generation of hydrogels are both feasible at a dosage of 30 kGy. QDs injected into the composite hydrogel do not alter its structure and the CdS QDs are of consistent size and distribution. To keep the QDs from clumping together in the composite hydrogel, the Cd atoms on the surface of the CdS QDs align with the N of the PAAm chains. Moreover, the cross-linking moieties present on the surface of QDs are used as a unique example to build QDs-hydrogel composites. Perhaps the most significant benefit of utilising QDs as crosslinking agents over conventional hydrogels is that they may generate numerous linkages in the gel networks [82]. In this strategy, QDs are integrated into hydrogels and can even alter their shape by virtue of the functional groups on their surfaces taking part in the cross-linking process. This means that tuning the moiety of the QDs and the monomer-to-QD ratio is crucial for controlling the composites' end results. In a practical water treatment procedure, Zhu et al. reported luminous hydrogels by incorporating CDs and lanthanide ions into a polyacrylamide (PAM) and polyacrylic acid (PAA) network [78]. Methacrylate functionalized CDs were covalently attached into the gel network through radical polymerization (Fig. 3 ), while lanthanide ions were stabilised inside the network by coordinating with the carboxylic groups of the acrylic acid (AAc). Changing the ratio of CDs to coordinated lanthanide ions might produce white light. In addition to describing the hydrogels' PL features, an energy-transfer mechanism was provided to explain their observed behaviour.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrogel fabrication by low molecular weight gelator-QDs assembly
Properties of quantum dots based hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: By incorporating CDs into the gel matrix, not only are the special qualities of CDs transferred to the hydrogel, but the cross-linked structure is also altered. Because of their fluorescence and low toxicity, CDs were thought to improve performance when incorporated into a hydrogel system by, for example, immobilising CDs' surface functional groups to boost fluorescence, supplying biocompatible carriers, etc. Yet, when CDs serve as the physical or chemical cross-linking sites of the gel matrix, the mechanical characteristics and self-healing capabilities of CD-based hydrogels are greatly improved.

Hydrogels that have CDs incorporated into them will take on the most alluring luminescent properties of CDs [94,95]. There is evidence that embedding CDs into hydrogel frameworks improves fluorescence intensity and longevity while also preventing the onset of agglomeration-aided quenching. The nanocrystalline QDs were successfully fixed within a photo-cross-linked poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG) hydrogel, resulting in a hybrid nano-system composed of inorganic and organic components [96]. The development of more robust photonic materials may be the primary benefit of QD-photo-cross-linked immobilisation. Preventing PEG macromolecules from expanding or undergoing structural changes is one such example. Nanoparticles may become isolated or clumped together as a result of matrix modifications. Alternately, a PEG macromolecule's cross-linking can undergo reversible, wavelength-dependant modifications when the nitrocinnamate moiety is used [97]. Chiral carbonised polymer dots (Ch-CPDs) that produce light are receiving a lot of attention due to their unique photonic features. Ru et al. reported the successful synthesis of red and multicolour-emitting Ch-CPDs employing a solvothermal method at a single temperature and the widely available precursors l-/D-tryptophan and o-phenylenediamine [98]. Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) of CDs has attracted a lot of interest due to their usefulness in a variety of fields, including data protection, white light emitting diode (WLED), and physiological imaging. Developing RTP probes with ultralong emission lifetimes in water is difficult. Using carbon dot-based silica composites (CDs@SiO2) (Fig. 5D, E) with emission durations as long as 1.64 s, Li et al. provided a practical approach to attaining ultralong RTP in air-saturated aqueous systems [93]. CDs@SiO2 is an exciting material for use in biological imaging because of its outstanding phosphorescence qualities, small size, and water solubility. Table 1 shows comparative studies amongst the reported several hydrogel based fluorescent sensors and their detection limits against the analytes.

Carbon dots (CDs) in a silica gel matrix were found to emit light at ambient temperature [99]. Both malonic acid and ethylene diamine were utilised as precursors in the synthesis, and this was done so that the CDs' surfaces would be particularly rich in C [double bond, length as m-dash] O and C [double bond, length as m-dash]N functionalities. In an aqueous dispersion, the CDs emit a bright blue fluorescence, and once incorporated into silica gel, they emit a green afterglow that is easily visible to the human eye. At 380 nm excitation, the phosphorescence experiments showed a lifetime of roughly 1.8 s, the longest recorded for CDs in solid-state matrices. For precise and on-site detection of Fe3+ ions in real-time, Zhou et al. developed a new ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on CdTe QDs doped hydrogel optical fibre [100]. Light is retained within the hydrogel fiber's core thanks to its core-cladding structure and step-index profile. Green emissive thioglycolic acid capped quantum dots (gQDs) and red emissive N-Acetyl-l-cysteine capped quantum dots (rQDs) are produced and placed into the hydrogel fiber's core. As Fe3+ ions infiltrate into the hydrogel matrix and preferentially extinguish the rQDs' fluorescence, the gQDs remain unscathed. In order to create unique nanogels with responsive features, the creation of tailor-made gelators has received a great deal of attention. We reported the construction of a fluorescent hydrogel based on S,N-codoped Graphene Quantum Dots (S,N-GQDs) serving as luminophore and sensitizer, taking use of the gel characteristics of nanocellulose (NC) due to its availability, sustainability, and renewability [101]. Increased fluorescence intensity was observed when comparing GQD solutions and hydrogels containing the GQDs. Wu et al. have developed a new fluorescent CQDs/hydrogel nanocomposite material (CQDsHG) that can detect Fe3+ with high stability and adsorption [102]. According to the findings, the synergistic performance of each component may be enhanced by using CQDs in conjunction with HG for the adsorption and quantitative detection of heavy metal ions in an aqueous environment. Optical detection and effective ion separation of quintet heavy metal ions have been reported using an agarose hydrogel anchored with carbon dots (CD) as a thin film solid sensing substrate [103]. The existence of chitosan moiety in the Agr/CD hydrogel network was supported by the observation of a strong peak at 206 nm (typical of chitosan) in the UV–visible spectra of Agr/CD, which was missing in Agr. The hydrogel films were then submitted to a photoluminescence (PL) examination, in which excitation wavelengths of 340, 360, 380, 400, 420, and 440 nm were used. Maximum PL intensity was observed in both hydrogel films at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Spin-assisted layer-by-layer assembly was used to create responsive photoluminescent hybrid materials containing immobilised QDs, as described by Kharlampieva et al. [104]. Poly(allylamine hydrochloride) (PAH) and poly(sodium 4-styrenesulfonate) (PSS) are examples of strongly interacting polyelectrolytes that can be used to confine thioglycolic acid-stabilized CdTe nanoparticles, while a poly(methacrylic acid) (PMAA) hydrogel matrix presents an elastomeric network with pH-responsive properties. This hybrid hydrogel matrix has quantum dot layers enclosed in PSS-PAH bilayers. Changes in pH cause reversible shifts in the system's photoluminescent intensity. With excess negative charge at high pH, the photoluminescent intensity of the hybrid matrix is decreased, whereas in excess positive charge at low pH, the photoluminescent intensity significantly increases. Incorporating QDs into hydrogel-LbL assemblies paves the path for a unique design of materials with pH-triggered optical features that might be refined into pH- or chemical-sensing devices. Aside from that, the author also revealed a unique 3D porous fluorescent hydrogel made up of CDs and cellulose nanofiber (CNF) [105]. A single, distinct peak at 355 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectra of the porous hydrogel may be attributed to the n-* transition of the carbonyl group from the CDs. Porous hydrogel's excitation and emission spectra for PL are displayed in Fig. 2e. The best wavelengths for excitation and emission were 350 nm and 450 nm, respectively. In addition, the photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of porous hydrogel after excitation with light of varying wavelengths to detect laccase, a new, cost-effective fluorometric platform was developed using sulfur- and nitrogen-codoped graphene quantum dots embedded in nanocellulosic hydrogels. By integrating lanthanide ions and carbon dots (CD) into a network of polyacrylamide and poly(vinyl alcohol), luminous hydrogels were created utilising a simple aqueous technique (acrylic acid). In order to achieve white luminescence, the ratio of CD (which emits blue light) to lanthanide ions (which generate green and red light) was fine-tuned [78]. The luminous hydrogel displayed chromic response to several stimuli, such as pH, organic vapours, transition-metal ions, and temperature, by capitalising on the combined specific sensitivities of the many emitters. The hydrogel's white-light emission and stretchability were further shown by a 400% fracture strain. Hydrothermal synthesis of ammonium hydrogen citrate was used to produce the carbon dots (CDs). The solvent casting technique was used to create the chitosan-based nanocomposites with varying concentrations of CDs [106]. The chitosan/CDs nanocomposite prepared with a concentration of 1.0 wt% carbon dots held the best optical, biological, and mechanical features. The results further show that the suggested chitosan/CDs nanocomposites exhibited remarkable pH-sensitive characteristics. For the detection of miR-21 in MCF-7 cancer cells, Mohammadi et al. created carbon dots-chitosan nanocomposite hydrogels by reacting carbon dots generated from different aldehyde precursors with chitosan that had been functionalized with ssDNA probe [107]. Moreover, schiff base reaction (creating imine bonds) between the amine in chitosan and aldehyde groups on the surface of the CDs was used to create three luminous hydrogels. Such hydrogels are easily generated in aqueous solution by creating an imine link between chitosan and aldehyde functional groups of the CDs surface. Hydrogen bonding allows for the formation of chitosan-CDs-EDTA complexes, which are comprised of chitosan molecules with -NH2/-OH groups, CDs molecules with -COH groups, and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) molecules with -COOH groups.

Most conventional hydrogels rely on chemical crosslinking of polymer chains to form the 3D structure. The networks in conventional hydrogel are not homogeneous because of the asymmetry in the location of the crosslinking spots and the extensive dispersion of the long chain forging. Hydrogels have low dissipative capacity to stress and mechanical properties because their structural change is tightly limited. Hence, there has been a lot of interest in recent years in finding ways to make hydrogels more durable. [118,119] Topological cross-linking, double-network, and nanomaterials-gel composites are just a few of the novel polymeric hydrogels that have been intensively studied for their exceptional mechanical properties in recent years. Incorporating nanoparticles with numerous surface-active groups into hydrogels, for instance, can effectively boost the mechanical property of hydrogels, resulting in nanocomposite hydrogels with high tensile strength, as well as remarkable swelling capabilities.

There has been report of using alginate-derived nitrogen-doped CDs as a drug carrier and a toughening agent for hydrogels using a process that involves the assistance of a microwave [71]. During the process of gelation, the Discs were spread out throughout the matrix at various locations. Fig. 6 shows a digital picture of fluorescent hydrogel, which may be found in the previous figure. It is evident from the digital image that the fluorescent intensity of the hybrid hydrogels raised with CD concentrations, which indicates trapping of CDs inside the gel matrix. This was demonstrated by the fact that the fluorescent intensity increased. In the presence of an initiator, the pathway of free-radical copolymerization is followed by both methacylamide and acrylic acid, which are both examples of vinylic monomers.

Carbon dots (ACDs) generated from Apium graveolens have been synthesised by a one-step hydrothermal procedure that does not need the use of any particularly potent agents or ligands. Capturing ACDs by an in situ gelation process resulted in a semi-interpenetrating polymer network structure with mechanical resilience, fluorescence behaviour, and natural adhesivity [29]. Hydrogels reinforced with ACDs have been shown to be elastomerically robust through testing in which they were subjected to high levels of uniaxial stress, repeated mechanical stretching, thixotropy, low creep, and rapid strain recovery. CDs and other gelators can build CD-based hydrogels via non-covalent physical contact and covalent chemical bonding enabled by CDs' surface functional groups; these hydrogels can then drive the sol-gel process and improve the hydrogels' mechanical quality. Hydrogel nanocomposites of polyacrylamide (PAM) and carbon nanodots (C-dots) were synthesised by free radical polymerization with C-dots serving as cross-linkers. Due to the strong hydrogen bonding interactions between PAM and C-dots, the storage modulus and cross-link density of NC hydrogels were significantly improved over the pure PAM hydrogel [121]. The PAM/C-dot NC hydrogel had remarkable mechanical characteristics, including a fracture strength of 48 kPa at a stretch of 2940%, as a result of the robust hybrid polymer network between PAM chains and C-dots. C-dots also impart outstanding fluorescence capabilities to the resulting NC hydrogels, increasing their potential for use in a variety of settings, including those of medical diagnostics, catalysis, sensing, and optoelectronic materials. In another study, authors constructed a nanofiller and double-network (DN) structure co-enhanced carbon dots/hydroxyapatite/poly (vinyl alcohol) (CDs/HA/PVA) DN hydrogel for bone tissue engineering [122]. This hydrogel has excellent mechanical strength, fluorescence, and degradable behaviour. They also highlighted that the compressive characteristics of the as-prepared carbon dots-based hybrid hydrogel were optimised (Compression strength = 3.462 MPa, Young's modulus = 4.5 kPa). This is due to the additive impact of the nanofiller and the co-crosslinking of chemicals and physical forces. The link between the microstructural characteristics and mechanical performance of hybrid and double network gels is also extensively explored by measuring their water content and swelling ratio.

In biological studies and medical diagnosis, hydrogels are crucial. They are becoming more useful in bioanalytical and biosensing applications. This article provides an overview of the various hydrogels and the roles they play in various contexts. Hydrogels have several applications beyond biomolecule immobilisation and embedding, such as a responsive material, a component of wearable devices, and a functional material. Biosensing is a cutting-edge technique in the realm of medicine that makes use of various biosensors that are sensitive to biological molecules. The advantages of nanotechnology in biosensing include its high solubility, low toxicity, and great biodegradability, all of which have piqued the interest of researchers and practitioners. QDs-hydrogel composites are cutting-edge immobilisation materials that give biosensors robust performance in challenging environments.

Organic fluorescent dyes (OFDs) and rare earth elements (REs) are two examples of luminescent materials that may be included into hydrogels to produce fluorescent hydrogels [123]. Due to their hydrophobic nature, however, hydrogels often suffer from diminished mechanical qualities when OFDs are included into them [124]. The possible biotoxicity of REs may limit their biological uses, despite the fact that hydrogels based on REs have improved fluorescence characteristics, greater transparency, and mechanical qualities [125]. Using biomass CQDs manufactured from glucose, xylose, and glucosamine, Wang et al. presented a series of all-biomass fluorescent hydrogels made by surface functionalization the hydrogels of alginate and cellulose nanofibers (CNFs). CQDs' cross-linking activity resulted in composite hydrogels with strong fluorescence characteristics and noticeably enhanced mechanical capabilities [111]. For the first time, a solid sensing platform made entirely of biomass fluorescing hydrogel was employed to detect Fe3+ and Au nanoparticles (NPs) in water. This research offers a promising biomass hydrogel material for use in biomedical imaging, biosensing, and monitoring. Using a bienzyme (GOx and HRP) system in either an aqueous solution or a solid-state PAA film crosslinked with DAPEG, a ratiometric fluorescence technique was devised for glucose detection using CD and Rh6G fluorophores [117]. When the glucose concentration was raised in either the aqueous or solid-state systems, the fluorescence of the blue-emitting CDs was quenched, whereas the green-emitting Rh6G was inert to the bienzymatic reaction of GOx and HRP with glucose. Good sensitivity, selectivity, and stability in glucose detection were displayed by the adjusted aqueous solution and film. Specifically, the dry solid-state film may be transported and utilised wherever blood samples are required. An array-based biosensor system was described by Jang et al. for the detection of phenol [126]. Photopatterning of a solution including poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG) hydrogel, PEG diacrylate (PEG-DA), photoinitiator, tyrosinase, and CdSe/ZnS QDs yielded microarrays (QDs). Hydrogel microarrays were made fluorescent and phenol-responsive by entrapping tyrosinase and QDs during photo-induced crosslinking. Enzyme-catalysed phenol oxidation by the entrapped tyrosinase led to the formation of quinones, which muted the fluorescence of QDs in the hydrogel microarray. The detection limit of this system was determined to be 1.0 M, and the fluorescence intensity of the hydrogel microarrays dropped linearly with increasing phenol content. Carbon dots-chitosan nanocomposite hydrogels have been made from several aldehyde precursors by reacting carbon dots with chitosan [107]. To detect microRNA-21 in MCF-7 cancer cells, a ssDNA probe was added to the assay. By combining the aldehyde groups on the surface of the CDs with the amine in the chitosan, three luminous hydrogels were created. UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were also used to characterise hydrogel films, CDs, and CDs-chitosan nanocomposite hydrogels. An enzyme-encapsulating CdTe QD gel was created by Yuan et al. for use as a flexible platform for biosensors [127]. Sol-gel switching was also seen in the as-prepared QD gels, and the gelation duration of the CdTe QDs was suitably decreased to several days by using a neutral phosphate buffer (PB) to dissolve the precipitated mercaptosuccinic acid (MSA)-capped CdTe QDs. In addition, CdTe QD hydrogels encasing the enzyme tyrosinase (TRS) were produced and used for dopamine biosensing. Enzyme bioreceptors (such as glucose oxidase or horseradish peroxidase) and fluorescent reporters have been encapsulated within a self-assembled peptide hydrogel composed of Fmoc-diphenylalanine and used as a biosensing platform (e.g., CdTe and CdSe QDs) [128]. Mixing water solution containing QDs and enzymes with monomeric peptide (Fmoc-diphenylalanine) solution was all it took to physically immobilise them within the hydrogel matrix in situ in a single step. They satisfactorily used a photoluminescence quenching of the hybridised QDs to detect analytes such as glucose and hazardous phenolic chemicals in the peptide hydrogel. When compared to traditional gel materials, the Michaelis-Menten constant (KM) of the photoluminescent peptide hydrogel was determined to be 3.12 mM (GOx for glucose) and 0.82 mM (HRP for hydroquinone). Based on these findings, peptide hydrogels may serve as an alternative optical biosensing platform due to their high encapsulation efficiencies for fluorescent reporters and bioreceptors, ease of production by self-assembly, and effective diffusion of target analytes.

In order to go further with device development and translation to wearable technology, biosensors must be immobilised in or on a functioning material. In a preliminary step towards such implementing the proposed system, Chen et al. discuss the fabrication and evaluation of an immobilised quantum dot-transcription factor-nucleic acid complex for progesterone detection. The sensor, which consists of a polyhistidine-tagged transcription factor, a quantum dot, and fluorophore-modified cognate DNA, is immobilised in a hydrogel [129]. The sensor was exposed to a progesterone (PRG) solution and dialyzed for 12 h (Fig. 7A ,B). The hormone progesterone causes the transcription factor to release its binding to its target DNA sequence (Fig. 7C,D). After measuring progesterone levels, the sensor was dialyzed overnight against HEPES to eliminate the hormone. It was a perpetual cycle. During progesterone administration and withdrawal cycles, fluorescent intensity was measured. Before the emission detector, a 450 nm long-pass filter was used to measure the fluorescence intensity from 550 to 750 nm (exc. = 400 nm) (Fig. 7E). According to the manual, we corrected for the baseline and reported the intensity in relative fluorescence units.

Microfluidic devices were created by Jang et al., and they include arrays of hydrogel microstructures that trap QD-enzyme conjugates within their microchannels [131]. The detection of glucose and alcohol was studied utilising glucose oxidase or alcohol oxidase-conjugated QDs within the microfluidic devices on the basis that H2O2, a result of different oxidase-catalysed processes, may efficiently quench the photoluminescence of QDs. The model oxidase enzymes, glucose oxidase (GOX) and alcohol oxidase (AOX), were coupled to carboxyl-terminated CdSe/ZnS QDs and entrapped inside the hydrogel microstructures to create a fluorescent hydrogel microarray that was sensitive to glucose or alcohol. Hydrogel-encased GOX and AOX stimulated the oxidation of glucose and alcohol, respectively, to generate H2O2, which then quenched the fluorescence of the attached QDs. As expected, the hydrogel microstructures' fluorescence intensity decreased with increasing glucose and alcohol concentrations, leading to a detection limit of 50 µM for glucose and 70 µM for alcohol in this system. Our innovative microfluidic device comprised of numerous microchannels allowed for the simultaneous detection of glucose and alcohol since each microchannel could perform separate experiments. Hydrogel biosensors based on ureidopyriminone-conjugated gelatine (Gel-UPy) and diselenide-containing carbon dots (dsCD) were created by Won et al. for cancer detection. Hydrogel self-healing, conductivity, and adhesiveness are all affected when glutathione (GSH) or reactive oxygen species (ROS) cleave diselenide groups of the dsCD inside the hydrogels [132]. In tumour circumstances (MDA-MB-231), the Gel-UPy/dsCD hydrogels show much faster healing than under physiological conditions (MDCK). Moreover, dsCD degradation impacts electrochemical signals via breakage of diselenide bonds. Hydrogels treated with a lot of GSH or ROS have great sticky properties and can detect cancer in living organisms.

As a high lactate concentration is a useful signal in clinical diagnosis, it is crucial to devise a fast and accurate method for detecting lactate. Hydrogel microspheres based on QDs were created for the quick detection of lactate using a mobile device [133]. In order to make the hydrogel microspheres (Alg@QDs-LOx MSs), CdTe QDs and lactate oxidase (LOx) were encapsulated inside of alginate hydrogels (Alg). The Alg@QDs-LOx MSs with dense multi-layer network topology displays increased storage stability for lactate oxidase and anti-interference capabilities. High lactate levels (2.5 mM) in human serum may be differentiated using fluorescent microspheres in as little as 15 min. It's more practical and inexpensive because the outcomes don't call for bulky, specialised instruments; instead, they can be observed with the naked eye or monitored by smartphones. The lactate detection threshold is 1.25 M. An optical cholesterol biosensor based on a carbon dot/haemoglobin (CD/Hb) combination was created by Bui et al. [134]. Fluorescence amplification of CD, which is ordinarily suppressed by - interactions between CD and Hb in the CD/Hb complex, allows this optical sensor to detect cholesterol. Hydrophobic contacts between Hb and cholesterol are more favourable than - interactions between CD and Hb, causing CD to be released from the CD/Hb complex. With a limit of detection of 56 M and a reaction time of 5 min in human blood plasma, the CD/Hb combination allowed for selective detection of cholesterol throughout a linear range of 0 to 800 M. Detecting cholesterol in biological and environmental samples with a biosensor based on the CD/Hb complex is straightforward, extremely sensitive, selective, quick, environmentally benign, and economical. Rhodamine B carbon dots (R-CDs) dispersed in the matrix of boric acid (BA) were created as a simple, low-cost agent for the visual measurement of intracellular and serum glucose levels using a multimodal photoluminescence (PL) bio-platform, R-CDs/B2O3 [135]. The R-CDs/B2O3 PL spectra showed an excellent linear connection with glucose concentration, suggesting that the latter may be monitored in real time in vitro. It was also shown that R-CDs/B2O3 may be utilised as a portable test gel for blood glucose by monitoring the fluorescent colour fluctuations of the gel under a UV light and processing the green channel intensities of the colourful pictures with the RGB option of a smartphone. The ease with which diabetic people may check their own blood sugar levels is a major plus. Glucose in blood serum was also effectively measured using this approach. Recently, a carbon nanocomposite-based ultra-sensitive and selective Thrombin biosensor with an aptamer-recognition surface has been reported [136]. Using a nanocomposite of fullerene (C60), multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), polyethylenimine (PEI), and polymer QDs, screen-printed carbon electrodes (SPCE) were modified to create this biosensor (PQdot). Synergy between nanoparticles is made possible by the complementary properties of the individual components of the C60/MWCNTs-PEI/PQdot nanocomposite, while polymer QDs display advantageous properties such as high stability, high surface to volume ratio, high electrical conductivity, high biocompatibility, and high mechanical and chemical stability. C60/MWCNTs-PEI/PQdot nanocomposite's high amine content made it an ideal substrate for the covalent immobilisation of amino-linked aptamer (APT), which in turn increased the aptasensor's sensitivity and stability. The measurement technique was the Differential Pulse Voltammetry (DPV) technique using a probe solution. Reducing the peak current (IP) in DPV is the consequence of decreased electron transport at the electrode/electrolyte interface, which was caused by the binding of thrombin protein to aptamers immobilised on the transducer. Gum tragacanth (GT) nanoparticles, acrylic acid (AA) monomer, N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA), and fluorescein O,O′-diacrylate (FlA-DA) were used to develop a superabsorbent hydrogel biosensor for blood glucose [137]. Hydrogels with immobilised CdTe QDs and a bioreceptor made from the enzyme glucose oxidase (GOx) were developed. Due to the synergistic effect of the QDs and FlA-DA, the constructed biosensor exhibited strong fluorescence. Enzyme-catalysed oxidation of glucose by the biosensor yielded H2O2, a product that inhibited fluorescence emission. The glucose levels correlated with the quenching rate. As compared to traditional gel materials, the proposed system's detection limit and Michaelis-Menten constant (KM) are both significantly lower at 0.1 and 1.35 mM, respectively. In addition, this novel instrument has demonstrated good repeatability and accuracy with unprocessed, real-world blood samples. The findings suggest that this newly developed highly fluorescent biosensor with straightforward manufacturing provides excellent immobilisation of fluorescent transmitters and bioreceptors and may be useful as a diagnostic tool in the medical field. Well-functionalized superabsorbent nanohydrogel was made from the nanoparticles (50 nm) of gum tragacanth (GT) polysaccharide, which included immobilised CdTe QDs and glucose oxidase (GOx) [138]. Blood glucose content in authentic serum samples was enzymatically detected using the constructed biosensor, with good repeatability and accuracy. As QDs and GOx are so well immobilised in this superabsorbent nanohydrogel matrix, it may find use as a diagnostic tool in the medical field. Bottom-up and top-down methods have been used to synthesis carbon dots (CDs) and their doped equivalents, such as nitrogen-doped CDs (N@CDs), from a variety of precursors. Nanosized 2D carbon-based materials are gaining popularity because of their superior biocompatibility, ease of manufacture, water dispersibility, and functional potential. The use of a biocompatible modification to induce efficient defect sites in GQDs was also investigated [139]. An effective modifier used for graphene and carbon nanotube dispersions, lithium salt of 6-aminohexanoic acid (caproic acid), was applied to its nanoscale equivalent, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and its efficacy in biosensing and microbiological application was examined. Adsorption of such a modifier alters the nanomaterials' pH and charge, which may improve their sensing capabilities for GQDs and anti-biofilm properties. Dopamine sensing was used to assess the modified GQDs' potential to destroy Staphylococcus aureus biofilms. Due to a lack of available surface areas, photoelectrochemical (PEC) performances may be diminished if PEC active material and biological receptors aggregate. The issue was addressed by Hao et al., who for the first time included 3D nitrogen-doped graphene hydrogel (3DNGH) into PEC sensing [140]. By incorporating nitrogen into the graphene framework, 3DNGH not only demonstrated superior conductivity to that of 3D graphene, but also offered a large loading quantity for PEC active nanomaterial and biological receptors thanks to its porous nature. The hydrothermal treatment produced ZnO/3DNGH. Increased light absorption and faster charge transfer allowed ZnO/2DNG to significantly outperform ZnO/3DGH in terms of photocurrent under visible light. The enzymes were immobilised using ZnO/3DNGH, and the resulting PEC biosensing platform showed promise. The positive outcome of this study suggested that 3DNGH is an excellent host for further research on PEC biosensors for use in biomedical and environmental studies. It is of critical importance to ensure food safety that portable, quantifiable, and consumer-friendly sensors for on-site surveillance of organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) be developed (Fig. 7F) [130]. A carbon dot/cobalt oxyhydroxide composite (CD/CoOOH)-based fluorescent hydrogel sensor is built for accurately measuring OPs utilising a handmade transportable supplementary device. The orange-emitting CD/CoOOH composite is encased in an agarose hydrogel kit as a fluorescence signal indicator, where it can be amplified for detection, protected from interference, and made more stable. Hydrolysis of the substrate by acetylcholinesterase (AChE) yields thiocholine, which triggers the decomposition of CoOOH and enables fluorescence enhancement of the hydrogel platform. In order to reduce platform fluorescence, OPs can selectively inhibit AChE activity and thereby reduce thiocholine synthesis.

Understanding metabolic changes in tumour growth requires very specific detection of extracellular lactate. Here, we describe the systematic development of a fluorescent probe for biosensing and bioimaging of extracellular lactate based on QDs in a hydrogel matrix. Nile Blue surface engineering of the destabilised quantum dot sol allows for the monitoring of lactate in the presence of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide cofactor and lactate dehydrogenase through fluorescence resonance energy transfer, in addition to the self-assembly of the quantum dot hydrogel. Of the many tiny compounds found in biological systems, lactate is the one that the surface-engineered quantum dot hydrogel shows the most selectivity for. Furthermore, when self-assembled into a hydrogel, the destabilised Nile-Blue-functionalized QDs are able to encapsulate individual cancer cells, allowing for the selective detection and imaging of the extracellular lactate metabolism. These features also enabled the functionalized quantum dot hydrogel to be effectively used for the detection of metabolic drugs [141]. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) detection in real time has emerged as a crucial tool for the investigation of biological events and disorders associated with oxidative stress. Due to its low concentration, considerable diffusivity, and strong reactivity, H2O2 in live cells is still difficult to selectively detect and accurately monitor in situ. A bioinspired enzyme, nanosized Fe3O4 decorated on three-dimensional graphene - based materials (Fe3O4/3DG NCs), were prepared through in-situ growth of Fe3O4 QDs with the size of 5–7 nm on porous 3DG networks via a facile hydrothermal method, in which Fe3O4 QDs can possess intrinsic peroxidase-like activity to efficiently catalyse the electrochemical reduction of H2O2 while the highly porous 3DG networks offer large surface area and high conductivity. The findings of electrochemical measurements demonstrate that Fe3O4/3DG NCs have good catalytic activity towards H2O2 with great selectivity and sensitivity (274.15 mAM−1cm−2), a low detection limit (78 nM), a quick response (2.8 s), and exceptional repeatability. To further demonstrate the potential use of our enzyme-free biosensor, we utilised it to in vivo detect H2O2 produced from live cells by adjusting the number of cells and the concentration of the stimulating medication.

The key to preventing and controlling foodborne illness outbreaks is rapid screening of potentially contaminated items. For the purpose of quick and highly sensitive detection of Escherichia coli O157:H7, a new fluorescent biosensor was created. Using the double-layer channel and immune magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs), the suggested fluorescence biosensor was able to efficiently separate and concentrate the target bacteria, and then employ immune QDs and a portable optical system to detect the bacteria quantitatively. In the presence of high gradient magnetic fields (HGMFs), the immunological MNPs in the channel caught the bacteria and formed MNP-bacteria complexes [142].

DNA is superior to other options because of its biocompatibility, precise molecular recognition capabilities, easy programmability, and low toxicity [143]. Both chemical bonding and physical entanglement of DNA molecules are viable methods for producing DNA hydrogels. The polymers are covalently bonded by chemical processes, providing both environmental stability and high mechanical strength. Hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interactions, and metal-ligand coordination are examples of the non-covalent interactions that physical hydrogels rely on to hold together their components [144]. DNA hydrogels may be further classified into two groups, hybrid and pure DNA hydrogels, based on their chemical make-up [145–147]. Using DNA as a precursor, describe how to create hydrophilic blue fluorescence-emitting carbon dots (C-Dots) in a concentration of 2% (w/v). These dots showed great promise as nanotrackers in microbial research due to their very effective internalisation in pathogenic fungal cells, low cytotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility, and good PL stability. Candida albicans was used as a model for other microbial pathogens in bioimaging studies [119]. The synthesized hydrogels are a safer alternative to the currently used hydrogels for medication delivery that are based on hazardous QDs. Dopamine biosensing was also achieved using a functional electrode (DNA-C-Dot/ITO electrode) constructed from a DNA hydrogel. For the identification of microRNA-21 in MCF-7 cancer cells, Mohammadi et al. created carbon dots-chitosan nanocomposite hydrogels (Fig. 8 ) by reacting carbon dots generated from different aldehyde precursors with chitosan that had been functionalized with ssDNA probe. Moreover, three luminous hydrogels were made by establishing imine bonds (through the schiff base reaction) between the amine in chitosan and the aldehyde groups on the surface of CDs [107]. Biosensors based on glutaraldehyde, nitrobezaldehyde, and benzaldehyde-based nanocomposite hydrogels showed satisfactory linearity with MCF-7 cancer cell concentrations from 1000 to 25,000, 1000 to 25,000, and 1000 to 6000 cells mL−1, with a detection limit of 310, 364, and 552, respectively.

The study of intelligent DNA hydrogel represents a promising new direction in multidisciplinary study. The DNA and hydrogel properties of DNA hydrogel complement one another. DNA's biological properties are combined with hydrogel's skeletal support in this innovative material. In response to external factors (pH, temperature, enzyme, etc.), smart DNA hydrogel may change its state from gel to sol [148]. Because of its high stability, biocompatibility, and biodegradability, intelligent DNA hydrogel has found widespread use in the fields of biosensing and drug delivery. The next generation of point-of-care molecular diagnostics relies heavily on rapid, sensitive, specific, and user-friendly DNA assays. High sensitivity requires an enzyme-based DNA amplification phase, which is expensive, time-consuming, and technically challenging. To address these needs, a novel DNA assay platform based on the DNA-catalysed synthesis of quantum dot (QD)-DNA hydrogel was reported [149]. Resonance energy transmission in a three-dimensional hydrogel network is so effective that a limit of detection of 6 fM may be achieved without resorting to enzymatic DNA amplification. Even for a single-base mismatch, the QD-DNA hydrogel provides quick detection (1 h) with good specificity. The Klebsiella pneumoniae carbapenemase gene is one of the primary targets of drug-resistant pathogenic bacteria, and our study shows that the QD-DNA hydrogel may be used in a clinical setting. Using carbon dot-loaded silica nanoparticles covered with DNA-cross-linked hydrogels, researchers have created bifunctional composite nanospheres for detecting carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) and delivering drugs to specific areas of the body [150]. After loading doxorubicin, a potent cytotoxic impact was established in vivo, and extremely sensitive and selective CEA detection was accomplished in vitro. Stress-induced biomarkers for radiation exposure and carcinogenesis include the accumulation of potentially mutagenic abasic sites lesions in DNA. We show that detecting abasic sites in dsDNA may be done quickly and cheaply using a fluorescence-based technique. Copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs) were patterned using DNA. A greater concentration of Cu(II) in solution can be attributed to the presence of abasic sites in DNA, which prevent the production of Cu NCs. The presence of abasic sites in the DNA samples was detected by the fluorescence quenching of carbon dots (CDs) in solution, providing a quantitative trace of the free copper ions. The binding of Cu NCs to normal and abasic sites in DNA is quite different, and not just in terms of fluorescence [151]. DNA recovered from onion and HeLa cells, as well as plasmid DNA in linear and condensed forms, all respond sensitively to the presence of abasic sites in the nanomolar range, as shown under UV light. Visual inspection under UV light is sensitive enough to pick up on clustered abasic sites DNA damage, when many abasic sites lesions are present in close proximity in DNA. Detecting mutagenic and carcinogenic compounds is crucial because they pose a risk to all forms of life. The DNA-carbon dot based electrochemical biosensor developed by Majumdar et al. is the first of its kind, and it can detect carcinogenic nitrosamines such N-nitrosodimethylamine (NDMA) and N-nitrosodiethanolamine (NNO2) with high sensitivity and selectivity (NDEA) [152]. The sensing electrode (DNA/chiCD/GCE modified electrode) was made by first depositing chitosan carbon dot (chiCD) on the glassy carbon electrode (GCE), and then electro-statically immobilising DNA on the surface of the carbon dots. Differential pulse voltammetry is able to detect NDMA and NDEA because, in their presence, the absolute peak current rises.

Herein, we summarise recent developments in the use of fluorescent hydrogels for biosensing and discuss their variety of applications. The widespread use of fluorescent hydrogels was hampered by issues with sensitivity and quantification, despite the fact that it provided a straightforward visual readout. Most of the fluorescent hydrogels are recently composed of QDs which are easy to fabricate and could be stored for longer times without affecting their quantum yield. Thus, in our discussion we included all types of polymeric systems especially the biopolymers and their QDs-based assembly. The natural polymers that may be found nowadays are readily available, safe, and biodegradable. Variations in polymer quality and composition, however, might cause problems with repeatability. They are more complicated structurally and cannot survive under extreme reaction circumstances. Yet, synthetic polymers cannot be broken down by natural processes and are not as biocompatible as natural polymers. It is crucial for environmental monitoring when there is a dearth of laboratory facilities and trained personnel. Using signal amplification methods to detect even tiny compounds at the single-molecule level has recently been suggested as the most powerful ultrasensitive technology, which may considerably increase sensitivity. For target identification, a large number of bioreceptors were immobilised in or on the matrix. These open questions are, in our opinion, the primary reason the given study is still in the proof-of-concept phase. Research has not yet been successfully translated into a product that is utilised by patients or medical professionals. Extensive research on sensitivity, specificity, reaction time, repeatability, and durability (stability) must also be conducted, in addition to such proof-of-concept studies. These crucial studies have not been done to their full potential and should be a focus of future research. Other characteristics, such as biocompatibility, toxicity, and extractable components, should be evaluated in conjunction with the approval of in vitro and, much more so, in vivo applications.

The integration of various sensing modalities in multimodal biosensing exhibits significant potential for forthcoming advancements. The amalgamation of hydrogels based on QDs with nascent technologies such as nanoelectronics, biosensors, and wearable devices has the potential to give rise to multifaceted and easily transportable biosensing platforms. The implementation of such platforms would facilitate instantaneous monitoring, simultaneous analysis of multiple samples, and efficient screening of a large number of samples, leading to a significant transformation in areas such as medical diagnosis, treatment, and environmental surveillance.

Furthermore, the integration of intelligent materials, such as polymers that respond to stimuli, into hydrogels based on QDs has the potential to facilitate the development of biosensing systems that are self-regulating and can be activated as needed. The utilisation of quantum dot (QD)-based hydrogels has the potential to facilitate the controlled release of biomolecules, as well as to elicit responses to environmental stimuli or to exhibit adaptive sensing capabilities. This broadens the scope of potential applications for these materials. In addition, the incorporation of artificial intelligence and machine learning algorithms presents an opportunity to extract significant insights from intricate biosensing data produced by hydrogel platforms based on QDs. The implementation of this approach could potentially improve the precision and effectiveness of biosensing analyses, thereby expediting the process of diagnostic and monitoring procedures with increased dependability. In general, the potential of hydrogels containing fluorescent QDs for biosensing applications is highly encouraging. The advancement of next-generation QD-based hydrogels with improved performance and broader applications is expected to have a significant impact on various fields, including healthcare and environmental monitoring. This can be achieved through continued research and collaboration amongst multidisciplinary teams.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dr. Sayan Ganguly is a currently a postdoctoral researcher at University of Waterloo, Canada. Before that he was at Bar-Ilan University, Israel. He obtained his Ph.D from the Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur, India. His-primary research interests include hydrogels, polymer–nanocomposites, MXene-polymer systems, polymer composites for EMI shielding, 3D printing, and magnetic-polymer composites. He has published several articles, chapters, and books in international journals and publishing houses.

Emeritus Prof. Shlomo Margel received his PhD from the Department of Materials Science, at the Weizmann Institute in 1976. He completed postdoctoral studies at the California Institute of Technology and then served as senior scientist at the Jet Propulsion Laboratory at CALTECH. After that he worked as a senior scientist, and an associate professor, at the Weizmann Institute. He joined Bar-Ilan Chemistry Department as an associate professor in 1987 and was appointed full professor in 1994. Prof. Margel is a polymer chemist whose main interest lies in the fields of polymers, biopolymers, colloidal chemistry, surface chemistry and biotechnology.
